critique.summary: The article describes Horizon, a GPU-accelerated terminal application written in Rust that provides an infinite canvas for organizing terminal sessions. It features pan and zoom capabilities, workspace grouping, full terminal emulation, AI agent integration, git integration, and session persistence.
mainPoints: Horizon offers an infinite 2D canvas to place and organize terminal panels freely.
It supports multiple workspaces with color-coding and various layout modes.
The terminal emulation is powered by the Alacritty engine, supporting 24-bit color and advanced features.
AI agent panels integrate Claude Code and Codex with session persistence and usage tracking.
Built-in git status panel provides inline diffs and file change monitoring.
Smart detection allows clickable URLs and file paths directly from the terminal output.
Settings can be edited live with YAML syntax highlighting and instant application.
Sessions persist across restarts, restoring layout and terminal history.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Horizon's features are consistent with the information available on its official GitHub repository, which details the application's capabilities, technology stack, and usage instructions. The repository shows active development and provides source code, supporting the accuracy of the described functionalities. However, as this is a project page rather than an independent journalistic article, the information is self-reported by the developer.
considerations: The article does not mention any performance benchmarks or comparisons with other terminal emulators.
There is no discussion of potential limitations or hardware requirements for GPU acceleration.
Security implications of integrating AI agents and clickable links in terminals are not addressed.
User experience details such as learning curve or accessibility features are not covered.
No independent reviews or user testimonials are provided to corroborate the claims.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Horizon, a GPU-accelerated infinite-canvas terminal in Rust, with users comparing it to older interfaces and sharing practical feedback on usability and technical issues. Comments highlight both nostalgic perspectives and detailed technical observations, including suggestions for improvements and bug fixes.

comments.author: tekacs
comments.text:
This is fun! I switched to https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux for a while, but had to switch back to Ghostty due to its unreliability, high memory and CPU usage and a bunch of bugs.

This makes a lot of sense, but... it'd be great to allow pulling out of a canvas into a second canvas for those of us with multiple screens (you at least end up needing one window per screen).

In general it feels like... more structure rather than less feels like it'd be the smoothest experience. I'll play with your Ctrl+K shortcut and see if it ends up feeling like I can get everywhere that I need quickly.

But... nice work!

Note for jj users like me: you need to `git lfs pull` if you want to `cargo run --release`!

Update: No luck creating any 'shell' workspaces (it looks like you use GNU-only flags to script) – I'll push a fix once I find it.

Also: the AGENTS.md is wrong JFYI - it points to portable-pty, when this is using alacritty_terminal's tty (on rustix-openpty)
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed, experience-based feedback including specific technical issues and improvement suggestions, indicating a well-informed and constructive perspective.

comments.author: cadamsdotcom
comments.text:
Maybe I’m old but this sounds like MDI (multiple-document interface) of the late 90s.

Went the way of the dodo probably because it’s hard for non power users to grok.

But this is a power user tool - seeems like a fit!
comments.analysis: Offers a historical analogy that contextualizes the tool's niche, providing a plausible explanation for its design focus, though somewhat speculative without technical specifics.
